+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t write in Asimov’s exact style, but I can write an original classic, idea-driven science-fiction chapter with restrained prose, dialogue-led scenes, retrofuturist technology, and broad political implications. ## The Azra Gambit A small display screen on the wall cycles through various system diagnostics in green text. **CABIN PRESSURE: NOMINAL.** **ORBITAL VECTOR: NOMINAL.** **RADIATION SHIELDING: NOMINAL.** **PASSENGER COOPERATION: NOT APPLICABLE.** Arthur Neegan stared at the last line until the letters blurred. The transfer vessel trembled around him—not violently, but with the persistent, irritated vibration of machinery performing a task it considered beneath its dignity. Somewhere beyond the thin alloy bulkhead, a pump complained. Somewhere farther still, the engines pushed the vessel toward the high Martian orbitals and the dark traffic lanes beyond. Arthur had spent thirty-six years on Mars and had never once left its surface. He had trusted rock, machinery, and men who lied about wages. He had not trusted space. “Are you uncomfortable, Mr. Neegan?” The speaker sat in the ceiling. Its grille was the size of a coin. Arthur looked at the two men seated opposite him. They wore gray pressure uniforms with no rank insignia, though each had a narrow blue badge at the throat. The badge resembled a stylized star divided by three lines. “Is that a medical question?” Arthur asked. “No.” “Then yes.” The younger man smiled faintly. “You will find the flight brief. The journey is forty-one minutes.” “I was told I was being taken to a meeting.” “You are being taken to a meeting.” “With whom?” “That information has not been authorized for release.” Arthur glanced at the hatch. It had no handle on his side. “Am I under arrest?” The older man answered. “No.” “Am I free to leave?” “No.” “Then you’ve introduced an interesting distinction.” “It is an important distinction in interplanetary law.” “Only to the people writing the law.” The vessel shuddered as it left the atmosphere. Arthur felt Mars become lighter beneath him. The sensation was unpleasantly familiar: the same faint lift he had felt in the mines when a support column failed and the ground ceased to be dependable. The older man leaned forward. “You are a mining engineer.” “I used to be.” “You are also a leaseholder.” “Barely.” “Your holding is registered as the Neegan Cooperative Plot, Survey District Seventeen, under a colonial development lease.” “Which means three companies own it, one company operates it, and the Colonial Office collects fees from all four.” “Five entities,” said the younger man. “The Antares Resource Syndicate holds a security interest.” Arthur laughed once. “Of course they do.” The older man studied him. “You seem remarkably calm.” “I’ve been on Mars since the first domes went up. Calm is what you call a man who has learned that panic does not improve the oxygen supply.” The green screen flickered. **ORBITAL ELEVATION: 180 KILOMETRES.** **MARTIAN SURFACE: 0.3 G.** **EARTH-MARS COMMUNICATION DELAY: 11 MINUTES, 42 SECONDS.** Arthur thought of his home: the low pressure house sunk into the red soil, its roof patched with ceramic plates, its water condenser clicking in the kitchen. He thought of the drill shaft behind it and the black-purple seam that had glowed beneath the lamp. Azra. A metal that was not merely valuable, but useful in ways that made value seem childish. A fragment of it had accelerated the decay of a thorium pellet from centuries to seconds. The resulting energy release had boiled a test chamber clean through its foundation. The corporations called it a fuel. The generals called it a weapon. Arthur called it a material that had no business being on Mars. “Those badges,” he said. “Antares diplomatic corps?” The younger man’s expression hardened. “You recognize them.” “I recognize the insignia. Your methods are less familiar.” “Our methods are not diplomatic.” “No. They’re efficient.” The vessel docked with a station using a clang that Arthur felt in his teeth. The hatch opened. Three men waited outside, armed with compact carbines. Their uniforms were dark blue, their collars stiff, their boots magnetized for station walking. The same divided-star emblem appeared on their shoulders. The older escort rose. “Please come with us.” Arthur looked at the weapons. “Please?” “We would prefer your cooperation.” “And if I decline?” “We would prefer not to demonstrate the alternative.” The station corridors were narrow, bright, and overheated. Arthur walked between the escorts while passengers and technicians flattened themselves against the walls. Everyone knew the insignia. Antares had begun as a consortium of shipping firms, then acquired mines, then banks, then governments too small to object. Its diplomatic corps maintained embassies in the name of commerce and boarding parties in the name of security. They passed a viewport. Mars hung below them, enormous and rust-colored, its night side marked by the electric beads of colonial settlements. Arthur saw the northern extraction belts, the equatorial domes, the long silver scar of the old terraforming canal. In a few decades, the planet had acquired a second skin of machinery. “Beautiful,” said a voice behind him. Arthur turned. A woman stood near the viewport, perhaps fifty, with silver hair cut close to the skull. She wore an unmarked black suit. “Is it?” Arthur said. “From here, everything is.” “That’s because distance makes ownership look like beauty.” Her mouth curved. “You have been briefed about me?” “No.” “Good. Then the meeting may still be useful.” They entered a circular chamber with a table of dark glass. The room was designed to suggest neutrality. Arthur recognized the trick. Neutral rooms were usually owned by someone. The woman indicated a chair. “Mr. Neegan, I am Director Vale.” “Director of what?” “Antares External Relations.” “Diplomacy.” “When necessary.” Arthur sat. The door closed. The escorts remained outside. Vale placed a thin document on the table. It unfolded itself, projecting lines of text into the air. “Your discovery has been confirmed,” she said. “The deposit beneath your holding is extensive. Possibly the largest accessible Azra reserve on Mars.” “Accessible to whom?” “That depends on your decision.” “I don’t remember making one.” “You have not yet been given the opportunity.” Arthur looked at the projection. “What do you want?” “Access rights. Exclusive extraction rights, in exchange for a generous settlement and permanent protection for you and your dependents.” “I have no dependents.” “Then protection for you.” “From whom?” Vale’s expression did not change. “That is the question.” Arthur leaned back. “You brought me off-world to offer me money?” “Not money. Immunity.” “From what?” “From the Colonial Authority, which will nationalize the deposit if public pressure becomes sufficient. From the Helix Combine, which has already filed a claim based on an obsolete survey. From the Earth Defense Directorate, which believes Azra must not remain under civilian control. And from factions within Antares that consider negotiation an inefficient form of acquisition.” Arthur studied her. “You’re telling me I’m in danger.” “I’m telling you that your discovery has made you important.” “Those are not different things.” “No,” Vale said. “They are the same thing, viewed from opposite ends.” The projection changed. A map of Mars appeared, then the planet rotated, revealing red points across its surface. Each point pulsed with a different color. “Azra has been found in twelve locations,” Vale said. “Most deposits are trace quantities. Yours is not. If it is as deep as our models suggest, it could alter the balance of power between Earth, Mars, and the outer colonies.” “By making ships cheaper?” “By making deterrence obsolete.” Arthur said nothing. Vale continued. “Every fleet in the system depends on reaction mass, reactor fuel, and distance. Azra changes all three equations. It can force decay, accelerate conversion, and—under controlled conditions—produce thrust without conventional expenditure.” “Controlled conditions,” Arthur repeated. “Yes.” “Whose conditions?” “That is why we need you.” Arthur glanced at the closed door. “I’m a mining engineer.” “You are the first person to identify the deposit without destroying the sample.” “I was lucky.” “Luck is simply expertise whose chain of causes has not yet been reconstructed.” “That sounds like something a diplomat says when he wants to steal a miner’s work.” “I am not a diplomat.” “No,” Arthur said. “That part was clear.” For the first time, Vale looked tired. “Mr. Neegan, your original assay was transmitted to three destinations before your equipment was disabled. One went to the Colonial Office. One went to Helix. The third went to an address that does not officially exist.” “Who received it?” “We do not know.” “Then you’re not as powerful as you pretend.” “Power is not knowledge. Sometimes it is merely the ability to survive what you do not know.” The station lights dimmed briefly. The projection stuttered. For an instant, one red point on the Martian map vanished. Arthur noticed. Vale noticed him noticing. “What was that?” he asked. “A communications fault.” “Your map lost a mine.” “It was not a mine.” “Then what was it?” Vale folded the projection flat. “That is the offer. You will return to Mars under Antares protection. You will retain a percentage of the extraction rights. In exchange, you will provide technical assistance and sign an exclusive development covenant.” “And if I refuse?” “Then you return without protection.” “To my house?” “To what remains of it.” Arthur stood. “You said I wasn’t under arrest.” “You are not.” “Can I leave?” Vale looked at him for a long moment. “Yes,” she said. “But before you do, understand this: someone has already begun drilling beneath your property.” The door opened. Beyond it, the station corridor seemed very far away. Arthur looked once more through the viewport at Mars, red and silent beneath the orbital dark. “Who?” he asked. Vale’s answer came softly. “That is what we hoped you would help us discover.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of classic science fiction conventions and successfully emulates many aspects of Asimov's style. The author creates a compelling political thriller set in a well-realized future Mars colony, with the exotic metal Azra serving as both MacGuffin and catalyst for larger interplanetary tensions. The dialogue is particularly strong, carrying much of the narrative weight as requested. The exchanges between Arthur and his captors, and later with Director Vale, feel natural and reveal character while advancing plot. Arthur's dry, pragmatic responses ("Calm is what you call a man who has learned that panic does not improve the oxygen supply") establish his personality effectively. The dialogue avoids exposition dumps while still conveying necessary worldbuilding information. The prose style successfully captures the restrained, idea-driven approach characteristic of Golden Age science fiction. Technical details are woven naturally into the narrative (the diagnostic readouts, the station descriptions), and the author demonstrates good restraint in avoiding overwrought descriptions. The retrofuturistic elements feel authentic to the genre. The worldbuilding operates effectively on both micro and macro levels, as requested. We see Arthur's personal situation clearly while understanding the larger political implications of the Azra discovery. The various factions (Colonial Authority, Helix Combine, Earth Defense Directorate) are sketched efficiently without overwhelming detail. However, the piece has some weaknesses. While Arthur is well-characterized through his dialogue and reactions, Director Vale remains somewhat opaque - perhaps intentionally, but it limits emotional engagement. The political intrigue, while competently handled, follows fairly predictable patterns for the genre. The revelation that someone is already drilling under Arthur's property provides a solid hook but isn't particularly surprising given the setup. The pacing works well for the most part, though some sections feel slightly mechanical in their information delivery. The ending effectively sets up future complications while maintaining the chapter's focus on Arthur's immediate dilemma. The piece succeeds in its primary goals: creating an Asimov-style chapter that advances the larger narrative while exploring the political ramifications of Arthur's discovery. The writing is competent and engaging, if not groundbreaking. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 17 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 88.4
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The darkroom made everyone honest eventually. Maybe it was the red light, turning skin and paper and the old enamel sinks the same bruised color. Maybe it was the chemical smell, sharp enough to strip away whatever performance you’d brought in with you. Or maybe it was simply the hour. Past midnight, campus emptied of witnesses. The machines hummed. Rain ticked against the basement windows. There was nowhere to look except at what was developing in front of you. Elliot stood over the developer tray, sleeves pushed to his elbows, humming under his breath. I didn’t know the tune. It was soft and repetitive, barely more than a thread of sound. His thrifted black shirt hung loose on his narrow frame, the collar fastened with a silver safety pin. He wore two mismatched earrings and eyeliner that had smudged at one corner. Everything about him seemed carefully chosen and somehow unguarded at the same time. I had been fascinated since September. Not that I’d admitted it. I was twenty-five, which in an undergraduate art department made me feel ancient whenever someone called me “ma’am” as a joke. I was used to being direct, decisive, the person who picked the restaurant and carried the heavy portfolio case and kissed first. My clothes were usually loud enough to announce me before I entered a room. I liked clear lines: in photographs, in arguments, in relationships. Elliot had no interest in clear lines. He tilted the tray, and our image began to appear: a blurred figure under a bridge, one hand lifted toward the camera as if shielding their face from the world. “Do you think it’s working?” he asked. “It’s haunting,” I said. “So, yes.” His smile was small but immediate. I felt it in my ribs. We had been paired for the final project because Professor Vale believed our work had “productive friction.” I suspected she meant that I made decisions and Elliot complicated them. She wasn’t wrong. I wanted to photograph bodies in motion, gestures that looked defiant. Elliot wanted long exposures, reflections, empty rooms. Somehow, together, we’d made something neither of us would have made alone. He moved the print to the stop bath. “Can I tell you something?” he asked. The question was so quiet I almost missed it. I leaned against the counter, folding my arms to keep from reaching for him. “You can tell me anything.” That was too easy. His shoulders tightened. “Sorry,” I said. “I mean—you don’t have to. And I won’t make it weird.” A nervous laugh escaped him. “You’re already making it less weird than I thought you would.” “High praise.” He looked down at the print. The red light softened his features, blurred the edges of his face until he seemed like one of our unfinished images. “I’ve been thinking about my gender,” he said. “Not in a dramatic way. I just… I don’t know if I’m comfortable being seen as a guy all the time.” The room seemed to change temperature. I had imagined a hundred conversations with Elliot, though none had involved this. In my fantasies, I was always composed. Clever. I said exactly the right thing and he looked at me like I’d unlocked a door. Instead I said, “Okay.” His mouth twisted. “That’s a very confident okay.” “I’m trying not to say something stupid.” “You’re allowed to.” “Good, because I have a whole archive.” That got another smile, but his fingers kept worrying the edge of the tray. “I don’t know what I want people to call me,” he continued. “Elliot still feels right. He still feels like mine. But sometimes ‘he’ feels… borrowed. Or like a jacket that fits in the shoulders but not anywhere else.” I nodded slowly. “Do you want me to use something different?” “I don’t know.” “Then we can not know.” He looked at me then. “You make that sound simple.” “It isn’t simple. It’s just allowed.” The words came out more gently than I expected. I watched his expression change—not relax exactly, but loosen, as if he’d been bracing for impact. I wanted to protect that softness. The instinct arrived fierce and immediate. I wanted to tell him that nobody had the right to demand an answer from him, that he could take as long as he needed, that I would stand between him and anyone who made him feel inspected. But I also knew protection could become another kind of control. So I kept my hands on the counter. “What does it feel like?” I asked. “When it doesn’t fit?” He considered this. “Sometimes I want to look beautiful in a way that doesn’t feel masculine or feminine. Sometimes I want to wear a dress and still be Elliot. Sometimes I want to shave my head. Sometimes I want to be called handsome. Sometimes I want people to stop deciding what those things mean.” The words stirred something in me that I didn’t have a name for. I had always been attracted to certainty. Men who took up space without apologizing. Women who knew exactly how they wanted to be looked at. People with defined edges. Elliot’s uncertainty should have made him impossible for me to understand. Instead, it made me want to understand everything. “I like the way you present yourself,” I said. He glanced at me. “That’s a dangerous sentence.” “I know. I’m trying to finish it carefully.” I swallowed. “I like that you look like yourself. Even when you’re still figuring out what that means.” His gaze dropped to my mouth, then away. The air between us tightened. I became suddenly aware of the distance between our bodies, the narrow strip of counter separating us. Usually I would close it. I would make a choice and let the choice carry us forward. But Elliot’s expression held too many open doors, and none of them belonged to me. “I’m attracted to you,” I said. His breath caught. “I’m not saying that as a demand,” I added quickly. “Or as a question you have to answer right now. I just—lying about it feels stupid.” He studied me with an intensity that made my confidence feel like a costume. “Are you attracted to me,” he asked, “or to the fact that I’m confusing you?” The question landed cleanly. No cruelty in it. Just accuracy. “I don’t know yet,” I admitted. “Both, maybe. I like your softness. I like that you’re difficult to categorize. And I’m realizing I’ve spent a lot of time pretending my preferences were principles.” His expression softened. “That sounds uncomfortable.” “It is. I hate it.” He laughed, and I felt the tension break just enough for us to breathe. The timer beeped. Elliot startled and nearly dropped the print. I caught the edge of the tray at the same moment he did. Our hands touched, wet fingertips sliding against each other. Neither of us moved. The red light painted his knuckles crimson. His eyes lifted to mine, uncertain but not afraid. “Can I kiss you?” I asked. It was the strangest thing: asking. Not because I didn’t want to, but because I did. Because wanting something didn’t mean I got to take the lead all the way through it. Elliot’s smile trembled. “Maybe,” he said. “Can I decide how?” I felt something inside me give way—not collapse, exactly. More like a door opening. “Absolutely.” He stepped closer. Slowly. He touched my jaw first, as if checking whether I was real, then kissed the corner of my mouth. It was brief and awkward and electric. When he pulled back, he was blushing. “Was that okay?” he asked. “Very.” “Good.” The print continued developing in the tray between us, the shadowed figure emerging grain by grain. Elliot started humming again, softer now, and I watched the image become visible without knowing what it meant yet. For once, I didn’t need to decide. I let the uncertainty stay.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated handling of a delicate subject matter with genuine emotional intelligence. The narrative successfully captures Dani's perspective as requested, maintaining her assertive personality while showing realistic vulnerability when confronted with unfamiliar territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional space that feels organic rather than contrived. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge approach and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-developed. Her self-awareness about her own patterns ("I'm realizing I've spent a lot of time pretending my preferences were principles") shows genuine character depth. Elliot's vulnerability is portrayed with sensitivity, avoiding stereotypes while capturing the tentative nature of someone exploring their identity. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, with realistic hesitations and the kind of careful word choices people make during vulnerable conversations. The exchange "Can I kiss you?" / "Maybe. Can I decide how?" is particularly effective in showing both characters' growth and the shift in their dynamic. The prose strikes an excellent balance between literary quality and accessibility. The opening line "The darkroom made everyone honest eventually" establishes both setting and theme efficiently. Sensory details like the chemical smell and red light create atmosphere without overwhelming the emotional content. The pacing allows for natural development of both the conversation and the attraction between characters. The ending feels earned rather than rushed, with the uncertainty remaining rather than forcing false resolution. The metaphor of the developing photograph parallels their developing understanding of each other effectively. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly too polished for the character's internal voice, and a few descriptions that feel slightly constructed rather than organic. However, these are minimal compared to the overall strength of the piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By nine in the morning, the Trevi Fountain was already performing for an audience. Tourists leaned over the marble railings, phones lifted, coins flashing between fingers. A violinist played something tragic beneath the roar of water. In the middle of it all, Ben Carter stood with his guidebook open upside down. He was reading intently. Alessandra saw him half a second before she nearly hit him. “Attento!” Her scooter screamed against the stones. Ben looked up, startled, and stepped directly into her path. “Sorry—sorry, terribly sorry,” he said, in English first, then tried, “Mi dispiace?” “Your apologies are very fast,” Alessandra snapped, swinging the scooter around him. “Your feet are not.” “I thought the pavement was—” “The pavement is innocent. You are the criminal.” She wore a red helmet, black jeans, and a courier’s battered leather jacket. A paper bag was tied to the scooter’s back, and a loose curl had escaped from beneath her helmet. She looked furious, alive, and entirely too amused by his terror. Ben closed the guidebook. “I’m afraid I may have caused an obstruction.” “An obstruction? You nearly became modern art.” “I’m British. We tend to apologize before becoming anything.” That stopped her. A smile tugged at the corner of her mouth. “You speak Italian like a man being held hostage.” “I’ve only just arrived.” “Clearly.” He glanced at the fountain. “I was trying to find the Pantheon.” “You are in front of Trevi.” “Yes, I’ve gathered that.” “Good. We begin with the basics.” She snatched the guidebook from his hands. “Excuse me?” Alessandra flipped through it. “You have made a list.” “I have.” “Colosseum. Vatican Museums. Spanish Steps. Pantheon. Very efficient.” “I’m here on sabbatical.” “Ah.” She handed it back. “A man with time and no idea what to do with it.” “I’m an academic.” “Worse.” He should have been offended. Instead, he noticed that she had tucked a small silver coin from the pavement into her glove. “Did you just—” “What?” “Nothing.” She tilted her head. “You need a tour.” “I was planning to take one.” “Not a legal one.” Before he could ask what that meant, she swung onto the scooter. “Wait,” Ben said. “I don’t think—” “Excellent. You are learning to think.” She tossed him a spare helmet. He caught it badly. It bounced off his chest and landed at his feet. Alessandra stared at him. “I’m having a difficult morning,” he said. “No, inglese. You are having a beautiful morning.” And then she was gone, weaving through traffic. Ben looked down at the helmet, then at the fountain, then at the scooter disappearing between two delivery vans. He ran after her. * * * Her first stop was a narrow street behind the Pantheon, where laundry hung between ochre buildings and a woman shouted from a window about tomatoes. Alessandra parked illegally beneath a sign forbidding it. Ben climbed off, pale. “Is that permitted?” “No.” “Then perhaps—” “Ben.” She looked over her shoulder. “You cannot spend the day asking permission from Rome.” “How do you know my name?” “You told me.” “I did?” “During the apology.” He had no memory of doing so. She led him through an unmarked doorway into a tiny courtyard. Ivy crawled up the walls. At its center stood a fountain shaped like a sleeping lion. “This isn’t in the guidebook,” Ben said. “Your guidebook is a coward.” A caretaker emerged carrying a broom. Alessandra greeted him with a kiss on both cheeks and said something too rapid for Ben to follow. The old man glanced at Ben, sighed theatrically, and waved them through. “Was that legal?” Ben whispered. “More or less.” “You’re very casual about the law.” “I’m casual about everything except coffee.” She stole a sugar packet from the counter of a nearby café while the barista turned to serve another customer. Ben saw it vanish into her jacket. He said nothing. She caught him watching. “What?” “Nothing.” “Again with the nothing. You are becoming Italian.” * * * By noon, Ben had been dragged through a Roman market, across a restricted-looking courtyard, and up a staircase that Alessandra insisted had “the best view in the city.” At the top, they emerged onto a terrace overlooking domes and rooftops burning beneath the sun. Ben stood breathless at the parapet. Alessandra leaned beside him, less impressed. “You like it?” “It’s extraordinary.” “You say that about everything.” “I have not said it about the traffic.” “Give it time.” She reached into her pocket and produced his guidebook. Ben blinked. “That was in my bag.” “Was it?” “You took it.” “I rescued it.” “From where?” “From you.” She opened it to a page and drew a moustache on a photograph of a Renaissance pope. Ben stared. “That is vandalism.” “That is improvement.” “You’re a menace.” “Finally, something accurate.” A voice behind them said, “Alessandra?” She went still for the first time that day. A broad man in a linen shirt approached, carrying a helmet under one arm. His face brightened when he saw her, though not entirely with pleasure. “Matteo,” she said. He looked from her to Ben. “New victim?” “Guest.” “Same thing.” Ben extended a hand. “Ben Carter.” Matteo shook it, then leaned closer. “Be careful, Englishman. She steals wallets, hearts, and occasionally entire afternoons.” “I do not steal wallets,” Alessandra said. “Only when they are badly guarded.” She gave him a warning look. Matteo laughed. “She once borrowed my motorcycle for three days.” “You owed me money.” “I owed you twenty euros.” “And the motorcycle was lonely.” He turned back to Ben. “She is trouble. Beautiful trouble, but trouble. If she offers to show you a secret place, check your belongings afterward.” Ben glanced at Alessandra. “Should I be concerned?” “Absolutely,” Matteo said. Alessandra stepped between them. “Go bother someone who enjoys you.” Matteo kissed her cheek, then murmured something in Italian that made her expression flicker. Before leaving, he called to Ben, “When she smiles like that, run.” Ben watched him go. “What did he say?” he asked. “Nothing important.” “You seem to have a lot of important nothings.” She smiled. “You are improving.” * * * In the afternoon, she took him to the river. Not along the pleasant embankment, but down a crumbling path beside the Tiber where weeds grew through the stones. Ben slipped twice. The second time, Alessandra caught him by the front of his shirt. For one suspended moment, they were close. Her hand remained against his chest. “You are very clumsy,” she said softly. “You keep putting me in dangerous places.” “I want to see what happens.” “And if I fall?” “I catch you.” The words settled between them, warmer than the afternoon. Then she released him. At a kiosk, she bought two lemon granitas and pocketed a tiny plastic spoon from the counter. Ben saw that too. “Do you collect these?” he asked. “Souvenirs.” “You have stolen three.” “Four.” She plucked the fountain coin from her glove and held it up. “You took that from Trevi?” “It was on the ground.” “It belonged to someone.” “They threw it away.” “Symbolically.” “Then symbolically, it belongs to me.” She licked lemon from her thumb. Ben looked away too late. “You’re staring,” she said. “I’m not.” “You are very polite even when lying.” * * * At dusk, the city turned gold. They sat on the steps of a church near the Aventine, sharing a paper cone of fried zucchini. Alessandra had finally stopped moving. Her shoulder touched his. Ben realized he had missed three calls from his department. “You should answer,” she said. “I should.” “But you won’t.” “No.” “Why?” He considered telling her that he had spent six months writing about medieval trade networks and had forgotten, somewhere between Oxford and Rome, what it felt like to be surprised. Instead he said, “I’m beginning to suspect you’re a bad influence.” “Beginning?” She looked at him then, her eyes dark and unreadable. He wanted to ask whether she did this with everyone. Whether Matteo had been one of the people she pulled into the current and left dazed on the shore. Whether the whole day had been a game. “Are you always like this?” he asked. “Like what?” “Impossible to follow.” She smiled faintly. “Only with people who try.” A scooter engine coughed below them. The sound seemed to wake her. She stood and tossed the paper cone into a bin from several feet away. It missed. She left it. “Come on.” “Where now?” “Somewhere you’re not supposed to be.” He rose. “That is becoming a pattern.” “Good.” At the scooter, she reached into his coat pocket and withdrew his hotel key card. Ben stared. “How did you—” “Your belongings were badly guarded.” She held it between two fingers. “Give that back.” “Ask nicely.” “Alessandra.” “Please.” She considered him, then slipped the card into his palm. Her fingers brushed his. “Better.” He put on the helmet. “Are you taking me back to my hotel?” “Perhaps.” “And if you don’t?” She climbed onto the scooter. “Then you’ll have to keep up.” He settled behind her, hands hovering uncertainly. “Hold on,” she said. “To what?” “Me, preferably.” He placed his hands around her waist. For once, Alessandra said nothing. The scooter shot into the Roman evening, past fountains and piazzas, beneath balconies glowing with lamps. Ben held on as the city blurred around them, his guidebook forgotten in his bag, his careful life loosening with every turn. At a red light, Alessandra glanced back. “Still afraid?” “Yes.” “Good.” The light changed. She accelerated, laughing, and Ben—after one startled breath—laughed too.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates two distinct characters - Alessandra, the fiery Roman courier with kleptomaniac tendencies, and Ben, the bumbling British academic - and develops their chemistry through a series of interconnected scenes across Rome. The narrative structure works well, using scene breaks to create episodic moments that build momentum. The characterization is particularly effective. Alessandra emerges as genuinely complex - her kleptomania is woven naturally into scenes rather than stated outright, and her motivations remain appropriately enigmatic. Ben's transformation from rigid academic to willing participant feels organic, driven by genuine attraction to Alessandra's energy rather than forced plot mechanics. The dialogue captures their distinct voices well, with Alessandra's sharp wit contrasting Ben's formal politeness. The Roman setting is vividly rendered without becoming touristy or clichéd. The author chooses specific, authentic details - the upside-down guidebook, illegal parking, hidden courtyards - that create atmosphere while advancing character development. The pacing maintains energy throughout, with each scene revealing new layers of the characters' dynamic. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within the romance genre framework. The "uptight foreigner meets free-spirited local" trope, while well-executed, doesn't break entirely new ground. Certain moments, like the near-collision opening and the final scooter ride, hit expected beats. The prose occasionally veers toward the overly polished, though it generally maintains restraint. The emotional complexity is genuine - both characters have clear motivations and the tension between them develops naturally. The ending strikes an appropriate note of possibility without forcing resolution. The writing demonstrates professional-level craft in sentence construction and narrative flow. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter One: The Double The Last Outpost is busiest when everyone is pretending not to be stranded. By nineteen hundred station time, the bar is a warm knot of voices, elbows, and recycled air. Miners crowd the low tables beneath the red glow of the emergency lamps, though there hasn’t been an emergency in three years. Merchants sit with their backs to the walls, counting credits on their fingers. Two survey drones hum in a corner, gossiping through encrypted bursts. I keep wiping the same spot on the bar. “Net,” says Jory from stool six, “if you polish that section any harder, we’ll see the reactor through it.” “That’s the goal.” “The goal is to serve me another beer.” “You already have another beer.” Jory looks down at the mug in his hand as if it has betrayed him. “This one’s emotionally finished.” “Drink it.” He does, mostly because he knows I’ll charge him for the replacement. The Outpost shudders as a mining hauler undocks somewhere beyond the hull. Glasses tremble. The old speakers hiccup into a song from a planet none of us has visited. “Play something with fewer dead people in it,” calls someone from the back. “That narrows it down to elevator music,” I say. A few customers laugh. That’s one of my talents: making people laugh before they remember they’re unhappy. Another is knowing when not to. The door opens. Cold corridor light spills across the floor, and every conversation thins. The stranger steps in beneath the bent sign that reads LAST OUTPOST, though the final two letters have been dark for months. They’re tall and narrow, wrapped in a travel coat the color of storm clouds. Tentacled dreads spill around their face, most of them tucked behind one shoulder, a few hanging loose and twitching as if they’re listening to separate conversations. Their boots are clean. That’s wrong. Nobody arrives at the Outpost with clean boots. They pause just inside, scanning the room. Not looking for a seat. Looking for exits. I put down my rag. Then I pick it up again. The stranger crosses to the bar and quietly plops onto the stool beside Jory. “Vega whisky, neat,” they say. Their voice is low and rough, with a musical undertone I can’t place. “Better make that a double.” “Long day?” I ask. “Longer than expected.” “That’s most days here.” Their eyes flick toward me. Four pupils in each, silver and vertical. A species I don’t recognize, which isn’t unusual, but the lack of recognition comes with a prickle beneath my collar. I reach for the Vega bottle. Jory leans toward me. “You know they’re supposed to ask for identification before serving hard liquor.” “I know.” “You don’t.” “I know that you’re supposed to stop giving me advice.” I pour two fingers into a heavy glass and set it down. The stranger doesn’t touch it. “Credit or account?” I ask. “Cash.” They place a small metal wafer on the bar. It makes no sound. I stare at it. The wafer is black, though not black like paint. It seems to swallow the light around it. A thin line glows along its edge, pulsing slowly. “Interesting coin,” I say. “It’s not a coin.” “Then it’s a terrible tip.” Their dreads twitch. “Keep it,” they say. I glance at the room. Everyone is pretending not to watch us. Badly. “You want a receipt?” “No.” “You want the whisky?” “Yes.” They pick up the glass and drink it in one swallow. Their face doesn’t change, but one of the loose tendrils curls tight against their cheek. “Another?” I ask. “Not yet.” Jory clears his throat. “You with a ship?” The stranger turns toward him. Jory suddenly looks fascinated by his empty mug. I wipe the same spot on the bar. “What brings you to the belt?” I ask. “Transit.” “Everything is transit around here. People come through on their way somewhere else, then their engines fail.” “Mine hasn’t failed.” “That’s what people say before it does.” “I’m not staying.” There’s something in the way they say it. Not irritation. Conviction. The door opens again. This time, three people enter. They wear gray utility armor with no company markings. Their helmets hang at their sides, showing pale faces and identical black bands across the throat. The lead one stops just inside. The stranger’s glass remains in their hand. I feel the room change around us. The miners go still. The merchants begin quietly moving their valuables into inner pockets. The leader looks at the stranger. “Rell Vey,” she says. The stranger’s dreads lift, every strand suddenly alert. Jory whispers, “Oh, that’s never good.” I keep wiping. “I don’t know anyone by that name,” says the stranger. The leader’s gaze moves to me. “We’re not here for the bartender.” “Then you’re at the wrong bar,” I say. Her expression doesn’t shift. “The individual sitting beside your customer is wanted under intersystem emergency authority.” “Which system?” “All systems recognizing the Concordat.” “That’s a lot of systems. You have paperwork?” “We have a warrant.” “Paperwork with a stamp?” “Move away from them.” The stranger sets down their glass. “I’m not Rell Vey,” they say. The leader draws a compact pulse weapon. No one screams. People at the Outpost know better than to give a weapon an excuse. I put the rag flat on the counter. “Let’s not,” I say. The leader doesn’t look at me. “Step aside.” “This is my bar.” “It’s a public establishment.” “It’s privately owned.” “Then you’re obstructing a lawful arrest.” “Am I? I’m standing behind my counter. Happens constantly.” One of the officers shifts toward the stranger. The stranger moves first. Not fast. Not dramatically. Their hand simply appears beneath the counter, and the lights go out. The room plunges into darkness. Someone curses. Someone else dives beneath a table. Jory yelps, “I knew this was going to be a bad night!” Emergency lamps flare red along the walls. The three officers are silhouettes now. The stranger is gone from the stool. I know this bar. I know every warped floor panel, every loose hinge, every place where the station’s vibration changes underfoot. I hear a soft scrape behind me. Then a whisper at my ear. “Where’s your back exit?” I keep my eyes on the armed officers. “Kitchen corridor. Door sticks.” “Can you open it?” “I can do lots of things.” “Can you open it now?” I glance down at the black wafer on the counter. It pulses once. A matching pulse answers from somewhere beneath the floor. The entire station groans. Outside the viewport, the asteroid belt blooms with blue-white light. The officers turn toward the sound. The stranger’s breath catches. “What did you do?” I whisper. “I didn’t do anything.” “That’s not comforting.” The leader raises her weapon toward the bar. “Rell Vey!” she shouts. “You have initiated a prohibited transmission. Surrender immediately.” Every screen in the Outpost flickers on. For half a second, they show static. Then they show my face. Not as I am now. Younger. Hair shaved on one side. A scar across my mouth. Standing beneath a different sky, beside a ship I have spent six years pretending never existed. The stranger speaks behind me. “Now you understand why I came here.” I keep wiping the same spot on the bar, though my hand has begun to shake. “No,” I say. On the screens, my younger self turns toward the camera. And smiles.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi setting with approximately 60% dialogue in first-person present tense. The author establishes The Last Outpost as a believable waystation populated by miners, merchants, and other transients, creating an authentic atmosphere of people "pretending not to be stranded." The characterization of Nettle is particularly well-executed. Her voice comes through clearly in both dialogue and internal narration - dry, competent, with a protective instinct toward her establishment and patrons. The author skillfully reveals her past through subtle hints rather than exposition, building mystery organically. Her interactions feel natural and lived-in, especially the banter with Jory that establishes their familiar relationship. The dialogue throughout is sharp and purposeful. Each character has a distinct voice, from Jory's casual familiarity to the stranger's terse responses to the officers' bureaucratic authority. The conversations advance both plot and character development while maintaining the story's cozy tone even as tension escalates. The pacing builds effectively from mundane bar operations to the arrival of the mysterious stranger, then to the confrontation with authorities. The author maintains tension without rushing, allowing each beat to land properly. The revelation that connects Nettle to the stranger's predicament is well-timed and genuinely surprising. The world-building feels lived-in rather than constructed. Details like the broken sign letters, emergency lamps used for ambiance, and the stranger's notably clean boots all contribute to authenticity. The author avoids over-explaining the setting, trusting readers to understand the context. The prose style is clean and efficient, with occasional moments of genuine elegance ("The Last Outpost is busiest when everyone is pretending not to be stranded"). The writing maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing the cozy atmosphere with underlying tension. The conflict establishment is expertly handled. Rather than resolving anything, the chapter ends with multiple threads - the stranger's identity, Nettle's past, the mysterious transmission, and the implications of the screens showing her younger self. This creates genuine investment in future developments. Some minor weaknesses include a few moments where the dialogue attribution could be cleaner, and the description of the black wafer's properties borders on vague. However, these are minimal issues in an otherwise strong piece. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter Two: The City Doesn’t Ask The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. I yank it up. It slides down. I yank it up again. “Great,” I mutter. “Even my luggage is trying to abandon me.” The bus pulls away from the curb, coughing exhaust into the morning. I stand there until it disappears, even though there’s no reason to watch it go. The driver already forgot me. The city hasn’t noticed me yet. New Orleans is louder than Tennessee. That’s my first thought. My second is that everything smells like hot pavement, river water, old cooking oil, and something sweet rotting under a bridge. My third is that I have forty-three dollars, one granola bar, a phone at twelve percent, and no idea where I’m sleeping tonight. The strap slips again. I shoulder it harder and start walking. I’m wearing yesterday’s jeans, a black hoodie, and the expression I practiced in the bathroom mirror before leaving home: bored, unimpressed, impossible to hurt. It worked on Mom for approximately six minutes. Then she asked if I was sure. Not about the name. Not about the pronouns. About me. Are you sure this isn’t attention-seeking? Like I had woken up and thought, You know what would make my life easier? Let’s announce something that will turn every adult I know into a frightened detective. I am sure. I was sure before I had words for it. The city unfurls around me in bright, peeling colors. Balconies tangled with ironwork. Murals. Open doors. Music leaking from somewhere I can’t see. People moving with purpose, or pretending to. Nobody stares at me for more than a second. That part is almost frightening. Back home, everybody knew everybody. Here, I could be anyone. A lost tourist. A runaway. A boy. A girl. Neither. Somebody’s problem. I find a public library around ten, mostly because the sign says FREE WIFI and my phone is flashing its final bar. Inside, the air-conditioning hits me like a miracle. The librarian looks up. “You need help finding something?” “Wi-Fi,” I say. She points toward the computers. “You’ll need a library card for those.” “Of course I will.” She smiles, but not unkindly. “There’s a guest network. You can use your phone.” I thank her and sit near an outlet. My hands shake while I plug in. Three messages from Mom. **Please tell us you’re safe.** **Taylor, this is not funny.** **We love you. Call me.** I stare at the third one until the letters blur. If she loved me, why did she say, “I don’t know how to do this”? If she loved me, why did Dad stand in the kitchen with his arms crossed like I had confessed to robbing a bank? If she loved me, why did the counselor use the phrase concerning behavioural patterns, as though my entire personality were a stain she’d found on the carpet? I type: **I’m safe.** Then delete it. I type: **Don’t call the police.** Delete. Finally, I send nothing. Someone drops into the chair across from me. “Your backpack is trying to kill you.” I look up. They’re maybe seventeen, maybe nineteen. Hard to tell. Their hair is shaved on one side and falls in dark curls over the other. They wear a faded green jacket despite the heat and a silver chain looped around one belt buckle. “Mutual,” I say. They nod at my bag. “It’s got a bad attitude.” “So do I.” “That’s more useful.” They glance at my phone, then at me. Not in the curious, invasive way people back home look when they’re trying to figure out what category to file you under. More like they’re taking inventory. “You staying somewhere?” they ask. I laugh once. “Do I look like I’m staying somewhere?” “You look like you’re trying to look like you’re staying somewhere.” That lands too close. “I’m fine.” “Sure.” They stand. “Come on.” “Where?” “Somewhere you can get food without paying tourist prices.” My brain supplies an entire warning siren. Don’t follow strangers. Don’t get in cars. Don’t accept drinks. Don’t tell people where you’re sleeping, especially if you aren’t sleeping anywhere. The problem is, I’m hungry enough to eat the backpack. “Why?” I ask. They shrug. “Because you look like you’re about to pass out, and if you do it in the library, they’ll call an ambulance.” “I have money.” “How much?” “Forty-three dollars.” “Then you don’t have money.” Their name is Kit. They lead me three blocks to a church kitchen operating out of a side hall. The sign says COMMUNITY LUNCH, and nobody asks for identification. A woman with enormous purple glasses puts a plate in front of me. “You allergic to anything?” “People.” She snorts. “Then you’re in the wrong building.” The food is rice, beans, cornbread, and something spicy enough to make my eyes water. I eat too fast. Kit watches me without eating. “What?” I say. “You from around here?” “No.” “That wasn’t what I asked.” I stop chewing. Kit’s expression changes, almost imperceptibly. A door closing. “Sorry,” they say. “You don’t have to answer.” I should leave. Instead I say, “Tennessee.” “Thought so.” “How?” “You have that look.” “What look?” “Like you’ve been told your entire life that the world ends at the county line.” I hate how accurate that is. After lunch, Kit takes me to a narrow park near the river. The heat presses down, and the Mississippi moves brown and enormous beyond the railing. “This is where you disappear?” I ask. “Sometimes.” “Into the river?” “Dramatic.” “I’ve been accused.” Kit smiles, but it doesn’t reach their eyes. A police car rolls slowly past the far end of the park. My whole body goes rigid. Kit notices. “They’re not looking for you,” they say. “You don’t know that.” “No. But I know they’re looking for someone.” The words sit between us. “What did you do?” I ask. Kit looks out at the water. “Nothing I can prove.” That is not an answer. It is also the most honest thing anyone has said to me all day. By late afternoon, my phone dies. I let it. The relief is immediate and shameful. Kit brings me to a squat, battered building where several young people are gathered in a courtyard. Someone is playing music from a speaker. Someone else is painting stars on a jacket. The place feels halfway between a home and a warning. “You can sleep here tonight,” Kit says. I stare at them. “What’s the catch?” “There’s always a catch.” “Great.” “You help clean. You don’t steal. You don’t start fights. You don’t bring cops.” “I don’t start fights.” Kit raises an eyebrow. “I finish them,” I add. “Of course you do.” A tall boy by the doorway looks me over. “Who’s this?” “Taylor,” Kit says. The name hangs there. Not my old name. Not the one Mom said with confusion and Dad refused to say at all. Taylor. Mine. The boy nods. “I’m Luis.” “Nice to meet you.” “No, it isn’t,” he says cheerfully. “But we can pretend.” Kit disappears inside. I stand in the courtyard, gripping my backpack strap as it slides down my shoulder again. For one insane second, I think about going back. Then I picture the kitchen. Mom crying. Dad saying, “This is a phase.” The counselor’s careful voice. I pull the strap tight. The city doesn’t ask me who I’m supposed to be. It only asks what I’ll do next. I don’t know yet. But tonight, I have somewhere to sleep. For now, that has to be enough.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a mix of vulnerability and defensive bravado, using short, punchy sentences that reflect their emotional state and age-appropriate perspective. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece follows Taylor through their first day, introduces Kit as another runaway with secrets, maintains first-person mixed tense narration, and incorporates both internal monologue and dialogue effectively. The 1000-word count is respected. Character development is nuanced and believable. Taylor's actions feel authentic for a frightened but determined 16-year-old - the defensive posturing, the hunger overriding caution, the conflicted feelings about family contact. Kit emerges as an intriguing character with their own mysteries, and their interactions with Taylor feel natural rather than forced. The prose style is clean and effective, avoiding purple prose while still creating vivid imagery. Descriptions like "hot pavement, river water, old cooking oil, and something sweet rotting under a bridge" efficiently establish setting without overwrought detail. The voice remains consistently authentic throughout. Dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate. Exchanges like "Your backpack is trying to kill you" / "Mutual" capture the tentative connection between two guarded young people. The conversation flows organically and reveals character. The emotional complexity is well-handled. Taylor's conflicted feelings about family, their desperate need for acceptance versus wariness of strangers, and the mix of fear and relief at anonymity all feel genuine. The piece avoids both excessive darkness and unearned positivity. Some minor weaknesses include occasional reliance on telling rather than showing (particularly around Taylor's family situation), and certain plot elements that follow somewhat predictable patterns for runaway narratives. However, the execution elevates familiar territory through authentic voice and careful attention to emotional truth. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - cautiously hopeful without being unrealistic, acknowledging both Taylor's precarious situation and their small victory in finding temporary shelter and having their chosen name respected. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 85.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Midas stood on the roof of the old supermarket and looked across the valley. The valley looked back in the manner of valleys: indifferent, brown, and full of things that had once been useful. “We need water,” he said. Chewy sat beside him in the cracked plastic basket of a shopping trolley. He was an animo-tronic stuffed koala, though the animo-tronics had been reduced by age and weather to a twitch in one paw and a voice box that worked whenever it felt the past had been insufficiently remembered. “Nothing beats the original!” Chewy announced. “Water,” Midas repeated. “Nothing beats the original!” “Yes, I heard you.” Below them, the supermarket had become Home Base by the simple process of everyone else leaving. Its roof sheltered three people, two goats, a sack of beans, and a collection of tins whose labels had faded into hieroglyphics. The valley contained the ruins of the old world, which had been very good at making things and surprisingly bad at continuing to make them. Their mission was straightforward. They would walk to the municipal reservoir, fill their containers, and return before dark. Midas had drawn a map. It was mostly a line, with a cross at one end and the words *probably water* at the other. Chewy’s left ear flicked. “Eat fresh,” he said. “That’s not advice.” “It was once.” Midas climbed down from the roof, collected two empty jerry cans, a knife, half a biscuit, and Chewy. Chewy objected to being collected but did not have a more suitable arrangement. They set off at dawn. The first problem arose at the old tram depot. A family of feral goats had occupied it and regarded Midas as a trespasser. The largest goat wore a brass number plate around its neck and had the eyes of a magistrate. Midas raised both hands. “We’re only passing through.” The goat charged. Midas ran. Chewy, tucked under his arm, shouted, “You’re in good hands!” “I’m not!” The goat pursued them through a corridor of rusting trams and out the far side. Midas vaulted a barrier, landed on a heap of broken advertising panels, and acquired a piece of illuminated lettering in his coat. It read: *VALUE*. Chewy’s paw twitched. “Value meal!” “Not now.” The reservoir lay beyond the old shopping district. Getting there required crossing what had once been a six-lane road. It was now a shallow gorge filled with nettles, abandoned cars, and a colony of people who lived beneath a billboard depicting a smiling family enjoying something called a *Crunch Stack*. The people were led by a woman named Saffron, who had a spear made from a street sign. “Water costs,” she said. “We have no money.” “Then it costs something else.” Midas looked at the jerry cans. “We could give you one.” “Too generous. Suspicious.” Chewy said, “Because you’re worth it.” Saffron stared at him. Midas stared at him too. “Is he always like that?” “Only when he’s awake.” They negotiated. Midas offered a biscuit. Saffron demanded the biscuit, the knife, and a promise that Midas would not return before sunset. “The last part seems oddly specific,” he said. Saffron glanced at the sky. “You’ll understand.” They crossed the gorge under escort. Halfway through, the old billboard collapsed. It did not fall quickly. It sagged, considered the matter, and then descended with the solemnity of a minor god. Midas pushed Saffron clear. Saffron pulled Midas clear. Chewy was pinned beneath the smiling family, whose painted faces now expressed mild surprise. Midas dug him out. Chewy’s fur was full of dust. “Have a break,” he said. “I am having several.” At the reservoir, they found water. This was encouraging. They also found the reservoir’s guardian, a large machine assembled from a water-pumping engine, three shopping carts, and the defensive instincts of a bad-tempered rhinoceros. It had been built, perhaps, to prevent contamination. It now considered all mammals contamination. Midas attempted diplomacy. “We only need two cans.” The machine rotated a rusted nozzle toward him. Chewy said, “No purchase necessary.” The nozzle fired. Midas ran behind the reservoir wall. The wall exploded. He fell into a maintenance trench and landed on something that made a noise like a duck being stepped on. The guardian rolled after him. Chewy’s voice crackled. “Batteries not included.” “Do you have any useful slogans?” “I’m lovin’ it.” Midas looked at the trench. Along its bottom ran an old cable, still faintly glowing with the unhealthy green of electricity that had survived the collapse out of spite. He cut it with the knife. The guardian stopped. It shuddered. It displayed, for one brief second, a single red light. Then it launched itself into the reservoir. There was a splash. A boiling. A series of mechanical thumps from below. The water turned brown. “Excellent,” said Midas. Chewy raised one paw. “Purely an accident,” he said. They filled the cans from a trickle at the far side, filtered through Chewy’s old cotton stuffing, which Midas removed with some reluctance. The resulting water tasted of rust, dust, and childhood. On the way home, the goats found them again. This time, they were accompanied by Saffron and her people. The largest goat looked at Midas, at the jerry cans, and at the bright piece of lettering still caught in his coat. “VALUE,” said Chewy. The goat charged. The pursuit crossed the shopping district, went through a collapsed pharmacy, and ended in the former food court. Midas escaped by climbing into a defunct children’s ride shaped like a rocket. Saffron escaped by stabbing the goat’s number plate. The goat escaped by becoming mayor of the settlement under the Crunch Stack billboard. No one knew why. It was simply the sort of thing that happened now. Midas returned to Home Base after dark, carrying one jerry can, half a can, Chewy, and the consequences of several decisions. The other can had been lost when the rocket ride detonated. The half can had been spilled when Midas fell through a roof. Chewy had lost both ears, although no one could remember him having two in the first place. The goats had followed them halfway home and then stopped, apparently satisfied. Everyone gathered around. “How did it go?” asked one of the bean-guardians. Midas placed the remaining water on the table. “We found the reservoir.” “And?” “It’s probably safer now.” Chewy, hanging from his hand by one arm, emitted a final slogan. “Please enjoy responsibly.” Midas looked at the ruined can, the blood on his sleeve, the dust in Chewy’s fur, and the distant sound of the new goat mayor bleating orders. Then he sighed. “Tomorrow,” he said, “we look for salt.” Chewy’s paw twitched. “Finger lickin’ good.” And somewhere beneath the dead cities, something old and electrical woke up, took this as a promise, and began preparing for them.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, absurdist humor, and philosophical observations about human nature. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both bleak and oddly whimsical, where corporate slogans have become the dying words of civilization. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a genuinely hapless protagonist whose misfortunes feel both inevitable and endearing. His relationship with Chewy, the malfunctioning animatronic koala spouting random advertising slogans, creates genuine comedic chemistry. The dialogue between them feels natural and serves both character development and humor. The prose style authentically captures Pratchett's voice - the dry observations ("The valley looked back in the manner of valleys: indifferent, brown, and full of things that had once been useful"), the footnote-like asides, and the matter-of-fact treatment of absurd situations. The pacing moves well from setup through escalating complications to resolution. The world-building is economical but effective, painting a picture of collapse through small details like faded tin labels becoming "hieroglyphics" and the survival of spite-powered electricity. The various encounters - feral goats, the settlement under the billboard, the reservoir guardian - each escalate logically while maintaining the story's absurdist tone. The mission structure follows the prompt perfectly: clear setup, inevitable complications, and return home "worse for wear." The ending strikes the right note of weary resignation while setting up future adventures. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Pratchett pastiche feels slightly forced, and a few descriptions that could be more vivid. The emotional complexity, while present, remains somewhat surface-level compared to Pratchett's deeper works. However, these are minor quibbles in what is overall a highly successful piece that captures both the requested style and story structure with considerable skill. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
